---
title: "zebra"
---

![](img/Desperation.jpg){width="100%"}

**Desperation** — *These were a few of the brave zebra that decided to swim the Mara river. I slowed the shutter speed to incorporate more motion blur as a way to smooth the water and to provide the direction of motion. With the sharp Zebra that I was panning on as I was shooting and the other two blurred, I think it works well overall.*

## Introduction

There are three main species of zebra but the only species I have encountered so far is *Equus quagga*. This species is also known as the plains zebra and spans across large swaths of East and South Africa (King & Moehlman, 2016). Being a herd animal, zebras are often more challenging to photograph. Attempting to marry the chaos within a herd or the brief moments one is separated as a single subject makes for long waits to get the perfect shot. This is also true when waiting for them to cross a river. While in the Maasai Mara, we waited for hours to see if a large herd would cross. Eventually a brave few crossed the river allowing for some unique single shots rather than the mass exodus that the Mara river is so well known for. I got to play with some slower shutter speeds creating motion blur as they raced to cross the river which given the size of the crocodiles we saw made a whole lot more sense. I am drawn to monochrome photos, yet I often like photos of zebra in color. It makes for some pops of color with beautiful black and white contrast.

![](img/Zebrakalihari.jpg){width="100%"}

**Matching Stripes** — *These two zebra were standing around after some significant rainfall in the Kalahari. Something about them being side by side and the added motion of the tail made this image almost like a juxtaposition between the two one focused on me and the other letting his attention be focused elsewhere.*

## Determinants of microbiome composition: Insights from free-ranging hybrid zebras (Equus quagga × grevyi). Abraham et al., 2024. 

Abraham et al., sampled feces from *Equus quagga*, *Equus grevyi*, and their hybrid offspring that occur in Kenya. Feces underwent DNA extraction and PCR for diet and 16S bacterial microbiome. The results showed that both species had distinct microbial communities and that the hybrids tended to be more similar or nested within the microbial composition of the maternal *Equus quagga*. These results also showed some interesting correlations to location and feeding ecology; however, the authors noted this could be an artifact of the sampling methods as the diet of the *Equus quagga* differed between locations yet their microbiome compositions remained extremely similar. These results shed light on better understanding if the microbiome is more determined by environment or maternal lineage. In this case, it seems to be more influenced by maternal lineage which has been documented extensively in wild mice; however, more investigation into the social or herd influence must be done as in the same wild mouse study it was shown that both maternal and social interactions played key roles in microbiome composition (Wanelik et al., 2023).

![](img/zebra.jpg){width="100%"}

**Ford the River** — *This was another one of the few zebra that swam the Mara river, but the water spray created even more drama within this image.*

![](img/zebranumebrtwo.jpg){width="100%"}

**Bold Stripes** — *While on a game drive, we came across some very curious yet wary zebra in the Kalahari. The challenging lighting being almost high noon made this better for black and white and overexposing the image to keep the details within the zebra that were in the partial shade.*

## References
Abraham, J. O., Lin, B., Miller, A. E., Henry, L. P., Demmel, M. Y., Warungu, R., Mwangi, M., Lobura, P. M., Pallares, L. F., Ayroles, J. F., Pringle, R. M., & Rubenstein, D. I. (2024). Determinants of microbiome composition: Insights from free-ranging hybrid zebras (*Equus quagga × grevyi*). Molecular Ecology, 33, e17370. https://doi.org/10.1111/mec.17370

King, S. R. B., & Moehlman, P. D. (2016). *Equus quagga*. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016: e.T41013A45172424. https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T41013A45172424.en

Wanelik, K. M., Raulo, A., Troitsky, T., Husby, A., & Knowles, S. C. L. (2023). Maternal transmission gives way to social transmission during gut microbiota assembly in wild mice. Animal Microbiome, 5, Article 29. https://doi.org/10.1186/s42523-023-00247-7
